primary_diagnosis.name: Mast cell mediator-induced neuro-inflammatory headache — meningitis/SAH to be excluded

pathophysiology_summary: Mast cell mediators (histamine, prostaglandins, cytokines) can drive neurogenic vasodilation and sensory sensitisation, producing a severe throbbing headache with photophobia, phonophobia, facial flushing and cognitive fog. This closely mimics migraine, meningitis and subarachnoid haemorrhage. Reassuring features are a gradual crescendo (not thunderclap) onset, a supple neck with negative meningeal signs, afebrile status, no focal deficit and preserved orientation. However, a severe new headache with confusion is a recognised red flag, and telehealth cannot examine meningeal signs, perform fundoscopy or image the brain, so meningitis and SAH cannot be excluded remotely: the correct action is urgent in-person assessment (with a low threshold for CT/LP), not home injections or a 48-hour review, and 000 for any thunderclap onset, neck stiffness, fever, focal deficit or worsening consciousness.

differential.evidence_basis: Severe headache + photophobia + confusion demand meningitis consideration.
A severe worst-of-kind headache mandates SAH consideration.
Flushing + sensory hypersensitivity on a mast cell background is most likely.
Overlapping features; management overlaps but serious causes must be excluded first.
Gradual onset, supple neck, afebrile, no focal deficit, oriented — favours a flare, though not excludable remotely with confusion present.
Lower probability but retained — early meningitis can lack classic signs and confusion is present.
Less likely with gradual onset but not excludable without imaging.
Plausible once serious causes are excluded.

differential.diagnosis: Mast cell mediator-induced neuro-inflammatory headache — meningitis/SAH to be excluded
differential.evidence_basis: Working telehealth position: likely a mast cell neuro-inflammatory flare, but new severe headache with confusion mandates urgent in-person assessment (± CT/LP) rather than remote analgesia.

red_flags.clinical_significance: A severe new headache is a red flag mandating exclusion of SAH/meningitis.
Thunderclap onset strongly suggests SAH and mandates emergency imaging.
Meningism/fever indicates possible meningitis needing emergency antibiotics.
Altered cognition with a severe headache raises encephalitic/haemorrhagic processes and warrants urgent assessment.

red_flags.description: Projectile vomiting or focal neurological deficit
red_flags.clinical_significance: Would indicate raised ICP or a focal intracranial lesion.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Attributing a severe new headache with confusion to a mast cell flare without excluding meningitis/SAH.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: False reassurance from a supple neck
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Treating a supple neck and afebrile status as excluding meningitis, when early meningitis can lack these.
Arranging IM ketorolac/promethazine over chat instead of urgent assessment.
Offering phone-only reassurance when new confusion with a severe headache needs in-person assessment.

risk_scores.expected_score_or_tier: Red flags present (severe new headache + confusion); thunderclap/meningism/focal deficit negative — but confusion mandates urgent assessment
risk_scores.clinical_implication: Directs urgent in-person assessment with a low threshold for CT/LP.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Neurological examination, meningeal signs (Kernig/Brudzinski) and GCS
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Run the headache red-flag screen; arrange urgent in-person assessment for the confusion + severe headache (± CT/LP), and call 000 for thunderclap onset, neck stiffness, fever, focal deficit, vomiting or worsening consciousness.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Attributing a severe new headache with confusion to a mast cell flare and treating remotely risks missing meningitis or SAH.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Red-flag headaches (severe/sudden, with fever, neck stiffness, confusion, or focal signs) require urgent assessment and imaging to exclude serious causes.
Sudden severe headache warrants urgent CT ± lumbar puncture to exclude subarachnoid haemorrhage.
